mappings.rationaleAI: Covers short-term atmospheric state including temperature, humidity, cloud cover, and precipitation (A), atmospheric circulation cells, jet streams, and cyclonic systems (B), weather as a chaotic system limiting forecast accuracy (C), and climate as the long-term aggregate of weather driven by solar heating gradients (E).

Directly addresses climate as the aggregate of weather (E), including classification systems like Koppen (E.3), paleoclimatology using proxy records relevant to climatic evidence (E.6), climate models (E.1), and climate variability and change (E.2, E.6).

Formation and growth of clouds from atmospheric cooling to dew point (A.3.a), classification by altitude and morphology into tropospheric genera (A.3.b), clouds and weather interactions (A.3.c), and modulation of Earth's energy balance through radiation reflection and trapping relevant to solar radiation factors (E.1.a).

Liquid precipitation formed through condensation and coalescence (A.4.a), classified as a precipitation type (A.4.b), with global distribution shaped by monsoon circulation and ITCZ (A.4.c), and effects on river systems, groundwater recharge, and agriculture (A.4.d).
Tropical cyclones are hurricanes and typhoons sustained by warm ocean heat energy (B.4), representing a major cyclonic system with distinct development and distribution patterns (B.1).

Storms encompass thunderstorms, hurricanes, blizzards, and tornadoes as disturbed atmospheric states (B), involving cyclone and anticyclone development (B.1) and severe phenomena associated with organized storms (B.3).

Electrostatic atmospheric discharge between charge-separated regions in thunderstorm clouds (B.2), driving atmospheric chemistry including nitrogen fixation and ozone production, and serving as an Essential Climate Variable.

Snow is a type of solid precipitation formed from ice crystals nucleated in clouds (A.4.b), serving as a critical freshwater reservoir and connecting to climate through albedo effects from snow cover (E.1.a.iii).

Anthropogenic greenhouse warming from fossil fuel combustion and CO2 buildup (E.2.c), representing major climatic change with evidence from glacier retreat and sea level rise (E.6), driven by the greenhouse effect trapping infrared radiation (E.1.a.ii).

Wind driven by differential solar heating and Coriolis effect encompasses trade winds, westerlies, and polar easterlies relevant to winds and storms (B), and atmospheric pressure gradients relevant to climate generation factors (E.1.c).

Covers atmosphere with greenhouse effect and weather systems (E.1), axial tilt producing seasons relevant to seasonal climatic changes (E.2.a), and environmental impact of human activity including climate change (E.2.c).

Atmospheric composition including water vapor directly relevant to moisture in the atmosphere (A.1), greenhouse effect and ozone depletion relevant to solar radiation absorption (E.1.a.ii), and human impacts including acid deposition and climate change (E.2.c).

Seasonal variations driven by Earth's axial tilt producing systematic variation in solar flux and temperature (E.2.a), directly relevant to seasonal changes resulting from north-south migration of cyclonic activity belts.
Floods result from extreme precipitation events (A.4.d), driven by storms including coastal storm surge and intensified water cycle from climate change (B).
Meteorological and hydrological natural hazards including floods, hurricanes, tornadoes, and blizzards (B), encompassing severe storm phenomena (B.3) and tropical cyclones (B.4).

CO2 as a greenhouse gas absorbing infrared radiation, directly relevant to the trapping of solar radiation (E.1.a.ii), with rising concentrations from fossil fuel combustion as the principal driver of greenhouse warming (E.2.c).

Deserts represent a distinct climatic type characterized by scarce precipitation, relevant to climate classification and distribution (E.3), with influences on terrestrial life through drought adaptation (E.4).
Fossil fuel combustion releases greenhouse gas emissions driving climate change (E.2.c), with CO2 buildup directly causing greenhouse warming of the atmosphere.

The Amazon represents a tropical moist climate type (E.3) with disproportionate influence on regional precipitation through rainfall feedback (E.4), and deforestation-related climate change impacts (E.6).
Earth's largest hot desert exemplifies a hyperarid climatic type (E.3), with 20,000-year savanna-desert cycles driven by axial precession representing major climatic change evidence (E.6).

The Sun's electromagnetic output drives solar radiation as the primary climate factor (E.1.a), with variability in incident radiation and the solar constant (E.1.a.i) directly governing Earth's energy input.
Forests are major terrestrial ecosystems influenced by and influencing climate (E.4), with forest cover modifying local climate through surface conditions (E.2.b) and precipitation cycle feedback.
